pattern: Two strings are anagrams exactly when their per-character frequency tables match, so tallying counts and comparing them decides it in linear time.
hints: Reordering letters never changes how many of each letter there are, so compare the letter inventories rather than the orderings.
A quick length check rules out most non-anagrams; otherwise count occurrences of every character in each string.
Build a frequency map for the first string, decrement it while scanning the second, and a mismatch or leftover count means they are not anagrams.

edge_inputs.description: one extra repeated letter breaks the match

stress.description: build two genuine anagrams by shuffling the same multiset of letters
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    base = [chr(ord('a') + rng.randrange(26)) for _ in range(n)]
    s = base[:]
    t = base[:]
    rng.shuffle(t)
    return [''.join(s), ''.join(t)]
stress.note: answer is always true by construction; exercises large equal-multiset compares

stress.description: mutate one character so the pair is almost-but-not an anagram
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    base = [chr(ord('a') + rng.randrange(26)) for _ in range(n)]
    s = base[:]
    t = base[:]
    rng.shuffle(t)
    i = rng.randrange(n)
    old = t[i]
    repl = chr(ord('a') + ((ord(old) - ord('a') + 1) % 26))
    t[i] = repl
    return [''.join(s), ''.join(t)]
stress.note: a single substitution guarantees a count mismatch, so answer is false
